title: Three CJNG Members Killed in Armed Pursuit on Colima-Manzanillo Highway
detail: On April 17, 2026, a citizen alert about three armed men on motorcycles near Coquimatlán, Colima — on the strategic Colima-Manzanillo highway corridor — triggered a coordinated joint response from Municipal Police, the Mexican Navy (SEMAR), the National Guard, the Mexican Army, the Colima State Police, and the State Prosecutor's Office. When security forces attempted to intercept the suspects, the men opened fire, initiating an armed pursuit. All three suspects were killed in the ensuing exchange of fire; no security personnel were injured. Colima State Security Secretary Lieutenant Fabián Gómez Calcaneo confirmed that the deceased 'belonged to a previously identified CJNG cell suspected of participating in various criminal acts in the region.' The confrontation occurred on the same corridor as the April 16 police corruption arrests linked to CJNG intelligence leaks at the Port of Manzanillo — suggesting a concentrated anti-CJNG operational push along the Colima corridor in the days following the Semana Santa security transition. The Colima-Manzanillo highway is a critical trafficking artery feeding CJNG's principal precursor import port.

sources.name: Colima State Security Secretariat (via RED Michoacán)

media.caption: Three CJNG members killed in armed pursuit on Colima-Manzanillo highway corridor, April 17

title: Michoacán Welfare Delegate on DEA Wanted List for Drug Trafficking; Disappeared April 2
detail: The DEA published a wanted notice for Rogelio Portillo Jaramillo, the Federal Secretariat of Welfare delegate for Huetamo, Michoacán, charging him with drug trafficking conspiracy in the Southern District of Texas. Portillo Jaramillo was last seen on March 28, 2026; his burned pickup truck was discovered on April 2 on a dirt road in Tiquicheo, Michoacán. The DEA classified him as 'armed and dangerous.' Reporting links him to a CJNG-aligned criminal organization led by his father in the Michoacán-Guerrero border region. The case is significant for several reasons: it demonstrates CJNG's continued penetration of federal civilian government agencies even amid post-Mencho enforcement pressure; it points to active cartel influence in the Huetamo-Tiquicheo corridor of southeastern Michoacán — an area of ongoing CJNG versus Los Viagras/La Nueva Familia competition; and the burned vehicle and disappearance suggest the individual may have been eliminated by the cartel rather than having voluntarily gone into hiding. The DEA wanted notice, listed publicly, signals active U.S. unilateral targeting even for Mexican officials.

media.caption: DEA wanted notice for Michoacán welfare delegate linked to CJNG drug trafficking
